title: TabICLv2: A better, faster, scalable, and open tabular foundation model
authors: Jingang Qu, David Holzmüller, Gaël Varoquaux, Marine Le Morvan (SODA / Inria)
tokenization_method: Three-stage in-context tokenizer over a (n rows x m columns) table. (1) Repeated feature grouping: each token bundles 3 columns via circular shifts, E1[i,j] = Lin(x_{i,j}, x_{i,(j+1) mod m}, x_{i,(j+3) mod m}) -> E1 in R^{n x m x d}; produces a fixed per-(row,group) token rather than TabPFNv2-style per-cell tokens. (2) Target-aware embedding (TAE): the label is added into the feature embedding of training rows, E2[i,j] = E1[i,j] + EmbedTAE(y_i) for i in D_train. (3) Column-then-row-then-dataset attention: TF_col (set transformer per column) -> TF_row (CLS token collapses a row's feature embeddings to one vector) -> TF_icl (test rows attend to train rows for in-context prediction).
feature_types_handled: numerical, categorical (including high-cardinality), mixed; no native temporal/time-series tokenization (i.i.d. rows assumed). Missing values handled via the standard TabPFN-lineage preprocessing rather than a dedicated token.
token_granularity: Per-(row, 3-column-group) token after repeated feature grouping; then per-row vector after TF_row CLS pooling. NOT per-cell (deliberate departure from TabPFNv2 to cut the cell-quadratic cost) and NOT per-whole-feature like a pure column-as-token model.
numerical_strategy: Standardization plus random rescale, then fed as scalars into the linear feature-grouping projection (no piecewise-linear or periodic embedding); continuous structure is learned by TF_col rather than a hand-designed numerical embedding.
categorical_strategy: Categoricals enter the same linear grouping/projection path as numericals (encoded as values), with category structure recovered by TF_col; there is no separate per-category learned embedding table for input features. Synthetic-data categoricals are produced by discretizing latent node dimensions via nearest-neighbor assignment or softmax.
high_cardinality_handling: For the TARGET (label) space, mixed-radix label encoding: for C>10 classes pick balanced bases [k_0..k_{D-1}] with k_i<=10 and prod k_i >= C, decompose each class into D digits, run TF_col / hierarchical classification once per digit and average -> arbitrary class count. NOTE: this addresses high-cardinality TARGETS, not high-cardinality categorical INPUT FEATURES.
temporal_handling: None. The model treats rows as exchangeable in-context examples; no patching, recurrence, or temporal fusion.
scales_to_many_features: Architecture is O(n^2 + n*m^2) (vs TabPFNv2 cell-quadratic). Reported: TabArena up to 100 features; scales to 1M samples and 500 features in ~450s using <24GB CPU + 50GB GPU via disk offloading. Cost is still quadratic in feature count m through the n*m^2 term, so very wide tables (thousands of features) remain expensive.
downstream_model: End-to-end transformer foundation model (in-context learner); no external GBDT or LLM. Inference = single forward pass over context (train) + query (test) rows, optionally ensembled over 8 column/class-shuffle estimators.
pretraining_objective: Supervised in-context learning on a large synthetic-dataset prior: predict held-out test-row targets given a labeled context, trained over millions of synthetic tabular tasks. Built on a novel DAG-based synthetic data engine (8 node function types: MLP, Tree Ensemble, Discretization, Gaussian Process, Linear, Quadratic, EM/cluster, Product; random Cauchy graph connectivity; prior filtering removes tasks an ExtraTrees model cannot beat a constant baseline on, ~35% classification / ~25% regression filtered).
key_results: Without any tuning, surpasses RealTabPFN-2.5 (the prior SOTA, even tuned+ensembled) on TabArena (51 datasets) and TALENT (300 datasets), holding top rankings across 10^3 to 10^5 samples. Wins on 600K-sample datasets where TabPFN-2.5 OOMs. Speed: 10.6x faster than TabPFN-2.5 on H100 at 50K samples/100 features; 11.8x faster on CPU at 10K samples. Total pretraining ~24.5 GPU-days (stage1 ~20, stage2 ~2.5, stage3 ~2).
baselines_compared: RealTabPFN-2.5, TabPFN-2.5/TabPFNv2, TabICL (v1), plus GBDTs and classical ML implied through TabArena/TALENT leaderboards.
transferable_idea: Target-aware embedding (TAE): inject the label directly into the per-feature/per-token embedding of context rows (additive EmbedTAE(y) term) instead of appending the target as an extra column. For a 70+-feature in-context or target-aware tokenizer this is a cheap, generic way to make every feature token label-conditioned and is claimed to mitigate representation collapse.
limitations: No temporal/sequence modeling (rows assumed i.i.d.); cost still quadratic in feature count (n*m^2) so thousands of columns are costly; strong architecture-prior coupling (TabICLv2 architecture on TabICL's old prior performs substantially worse, and vice versa) means the recipe is not modular; synthetic-prior + pretraining code not yet released at submission (only inference code + weights), limiting full reproducibility.
pitfalls: (1) Mixed-radix encoding solves high-cardinality TARGETS, easy to misread as a high-cardinality FEATURE encoder. (2) TAE only works where labeled context exists at inference (ICL setting) - it is not a drop-in supervised end-to-end feature embedding. (3) Headline 'surpasses tuned RealTabPFN-2.5 without tuning' depends on 8-estimator ensembling via column/class shuffles, which is itself a form of test-time augmentation cost. (4) Architecture-prior interaction means ablation gains are non-additive; component Elo deltas (~100 Elo each for synthetic prior / TAE / Muon / QASSMax) cannot be summed.
relevance_to_topic: Strongest current open tabular foundation model: shows how to tokenize and scale heterogeneous numerical+categorical features (up to 500) into a transformer via column-then-row attention plus a label-conditioned (target-aware) embedding, directly relevant to unifying 70+ mixed features.
code_available: yes (inference code + model weights) https://github.com/soda-inria/tabicl ; synthetic data engine + pretraining code promised but not yet released at submission
